questions.question: Which one of the following is NOT logically equivalent to ~exists x (forall y (alpha) and forall z (beta)) ? A. forall x (exists z (~beta) -> forall y (alpha))  B. forall x (forall z (beta) -> exists y (~alpha))  C. forall x (forall y (alpha) -> exists z (~beta))  D. forall x (exists y (~alpha) -> exists z (~beta))
questions.answer: D. forall x (exists y (~alpha) -> exists z (~beta))
questions.to_find: Which option is NOT equivalent to the given negated existential statement

understand.plain: Start by pushing the negation through the existential quantifier and the conjunction. ~exists x(...) becomes forall x ~(...), and ~(A and B) becomes (A -> ~B). The given sentence rewrites to forall x (forall y(alpha) -> exists z(~beta)), so any option logically equal to this implication (the statement itself or its contrapositive) IS equivalent; the converse and inverse are NOT.

keywords.explain: ~exists x P(x) is forall x ~P(x), and ~forall x P(x) is exists x ~P(x).
keywords.example: ~exists z beta becomes forall z ~beta, i.e. ~forall z beta becomes exists z ~beta.

keywords.explain: The negation ~(A and B) equals (A -> ~B), turning a negated AND into an implication.
keywords.example: ~(forall y alpha and forall z beta) = (forall y alpha -> ~forall z beta) = (forall y alpha -> exists z ~beta).

keywords.explain: P -> Q is logically equivalent to its contrapositive ~Q -> ~P, but NOT to its converse Q -> P or inverse ~P -> ~Q.
keywords.example: (forall y alpha -> exists z ~beta) equals (forall z beta -> exists y ~alpha), but not (exists z ~beta -> forall y alpha).

understand.visual_alt: A logical chain: the original negated existential is rewritten as forall x (P -> Q), whose only equivalents are itself and its contrapositive.

given.aim: Identify the single option that is NOT logically equivalent to ~exists x (forall y alpha and forall z beta).

terms.meaning: negation of an existential, pushed inward to forall x ~(...)
terms.connects: begins the simplification of the whole sentence

terms.meaning: the first conjunct inside the scope of x
terms.connects: becomes the antecedent of the derived implication

terms.meaning: the negation of the second conjunct forall z (beta)
terms.connects: becomes the consequent of the derived implication

terms.meaning: the implication form forall x (P -> Q) the sentence reduces to
terms.connects: lets us test each option as statement, contrapositive, converse, or inverse

given.plan: Simplify the sentence to forall x (forall y alpha -> exists z ~beta); then classify each option as the statement, its contrapositive (equivalent) or its converse/inverse (not equivalent).

steps.title: Push the negation inward
steps.apply: ~exists x (forall y alpha and forall z beta) = forall x ~(forall y alpha and forall z beta).
steps.note: The leading ~exists x flips to forall x with the body negated.

steps.title: Convert the negated AND to an implication
steps.apply: forall x ~(forall y alpha and forall z beta) = forall x (forall y alpha -> ~forall z beta) = forall x (forall y alpha -> exists z ~beta).
steps.note: This is exactly option C, so C is equivalent (it is the statement itself).

steps.title: Use the contrapositive to confirm B
steps.apply: Contrapositive of (forall y alpha -> exists z ~beta) is (forall z beta -> exists y ~alpha), which is option B. So B is equivalent.
steps.note: Statement C and contrapositive B are the two equivalent forms.

steps.title: Expose the non-equivalent options
steps.formula_raw: converse Q -> P and inverse ~P -> ~Q are NOT equivalent to P -> Q
steps.apply: Option A, exists z ~beta -> forall y alpha, is the converse Q -> P. Option D, exists y ~alpha -> exists z ~beta, is ~P -> Q, again not equal to P -> Q. The official key (and GATE) flags D as the intended NOT-equivalent answer.
steps.note: Both A and D fail equivalence; the marked answer is D.

solution.result: D is the option that is NOT logically equivalent to the given sentence.

questions.formula_note: Rewriting ~(A and B) as (A -> ~B) is the key move; only the statement and its contrapositive stay equivalent. Note both A and D are arguably non-equivalent, so the official key marks this question as excluded; the intended GATE answer is D.

questions.question: Consider the statement "Not all that glitters is gold". Predicate glitters(x) is true if x glitters and predicate gold(x) is true if x is gold. Which one of the following logical formulae represents the above statement? A. forall x : glitters(x) => ~gold(x)  B. forall x : gold(x) => glitters(x)  C. exists x : gold(x) and ~glitters(x)  D. exists x : glitters(x) and ~gold(x)
questions.to_find: The first-order formula for "Not all that glitters is gold"

understand.plain: "All that glitters is gold" means forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x)). The word "Not all" negates this whole universal. Negating forall ... -> ... gives an existential conjunction: there exists something that glitters yet is not gold.

keywords.explain: ~forall x (P -> Q) becomes exists x (P and ~Q).
"All A are B" is encoded as forall x (A(x) -> B(x)), not as forall x (A and B).
keywords.example: "All that glitters is gold" = forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x)).

keywords.explain: Denying a universal asserts the existence of one counterexample.
keywords.example: One glittering non-gold object makes "all that glitters is gold" false.

understand.visual_alt: The universal claim 'all glittering things are gold' is denied by exhibiting one glittering thing that is not gold.

given.aim: Translate "Not all that glitters is gold" into a correct predicate-logic formula.

terms.meaning: predicate true when x glitters
terms.connects: antecedent of the implication being negated

terms.meaning: predicate true when x is gold
terms.connects: consequent of the implication

terms.meaning: the positive claim "all that glitters is gold"
terms.connects: its negation is the answer

terms.meaning: asserts at least one counterexample object
terms.connects: appears after negating the universal

given.plan: Write the positive universal, apply the negation rule ~forall(P->Q) = exists(P and ~Q), and match to the options.

steps.title: Encode the positive sentence
steps.formula_raw: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: "All that glitters is gold" = forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x)).
steps.note: Use implication, not conjunction, for an "all ... are ..." claim.

steps.title: Negate the universal
steps.apply: "Not all that glitters is gold" = ~forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x)) = exists x ~(glitters(x) -> gold(x)).
steps.note: The "Not all" flips forall to exists.

steps.title: Negate the inner implication
steps.apply: exists x ~(glitters(x) -> gold(x)) = exists x (glitters(x) and ~gold(x)).
steps.note: This matches option D exactly.

solution.result: The statement is exists x (glitters(x) and ~gold(x)) -- option D.

questions.formula_note: The decisive identity is ~(P -> Q) = P and ~Q; combined with ~forall = exists it turns "not all A are B" into "some A is not B".

questions.question: The CORRECT formula for the sentence, "not all Rainy days are Cold" is A. forall d (Rainy(d) and ~Cold(d))  B. forall d (~Rainy(d) -> Cold(d))  C. exists d (~Rainy(d) -> Cold(d))  D. exists d (Rainy(d) and ~Cold(d))
questions.to_find: The first-order formula for "not all Rainy days are Cold"

understand.plain: "All Rainy days are Cold" is forall d (Rainy(d) -> Cold(d)). Prefixing "not all" negates this. Negating a universal implication yields an existential statement asserting one rainy-but-not-cold day exists.

keywords.explain: Denying a universal is equivalent to claiming an existential counterexample.
keywords.example: "Not all days are cold" = there exists a day that is not cold.

keywords.explain: "All Rainy days are Cold" restricts to rainy days via an implication Rainy -> Cold.
~(Rainy -> Cold) is Rainy and ~Cold -- a rainy day that is not cold.

understand.visual_alt: The universal 'every rainy day is cold' is refuted by one rainy day that is not cold.

given.aim: Translate "not all Rainy days are Cold" into the correct predicate-logic formula.

terms.meaning: predicate true when day d is rainy
terms.connects: antecedent of the restricting implication

terms.meaning: predicate true when day d is cold
terms.connects: consequent of the implication

terms.meaning: the positive claim "all rainy days are cold"
terms.connects: its negation gives the answer

terms.meaning: asserts one counterexample day
terms.example: a rainy day that is not cold
terms.connects: results from negating the universal

given.plan: Write the positive universal implication, negate it with ~forall(P->Q) = exists(P and ~Q), and match the options.

steps.title: Encode "all rainy days are cold"
steps.apply: The positive sentence uses an implication restricted to rainy days.
steps.note: Do not write Rainy and Cold here; "all A are B" needs ->.

steps.title: Apply the "not all" negation
steps.apply: "Not all Rainy days are Cold" = ~forall d (Rainy(d) -> Cold(d)) = exists d ~(Rainy(d) -> Cold(d)).
steps.note: forall flips to exists under negation.

steps.title: Negate the inner implication
steps.apply: exists d ~(Rainy(d) -> Cold(d)) = exists d (Rainy(d) and ~Cold(d)).
steps.note: This is option D.

solution.result: The correct formula is exists d (Rainy(d) and ~Cold(d)) -- option D.

questions.formula_note: As with the gold question, ~(P -> Q) = P and ~Q converts a denied universal into an existential "some A is not B".

questions.question: Which one of the following well-formed formulae is a tautology? A. forall x exists y R(x,y) <-> exists y forall x R(x,y)  B. (forall x [exists y R(x,y) -> S(x,y)]) -> forall x exists y S(x,y)  C. [forall x exists y (P(x,y) -> R(x,y))] <-> [forall x exists y (~P(x,y) or R(x,y))]  D. forall x forall y P(x,y) -> forall x forall y P(y,x)
questions.answer: C. [forall x exists y (P(x,y) -> R(x,y))] <-> [forall x exists y (~P(x,y) or R(x,y))]
questions.to_find: Which formula is a tautology (true in every interpretation)

understand.plain: A tautology must hold in every model. Option C replaces the matrix P -> R with the logically identical ~P or R under the SAME quantifier prefix, so the two sides are always equal -- a tautology. The others quietly swap quantifier order or change variable bindings, which can fail.

keywords.explain: P -> Q is logically equivalent to ~P or Q in every interpretation.
forall x exists y is generally weaker than exists y forall x; they are not equivalent.
keywords.example: "everyone has some friend" does not imply "someone is everyone's friend".

keywords.explain: Replacing a subformula by a logical equivalent keeps the whole formula equivalent, no matter the quantifiers in front.
keywords.example: forall x exists y (P->R) <-> forall x exists y (~P or R).

understand.visual_alt: Each side of option C has the same forall-x exists-y prefix wrapping the equivalent matrices P->R and ~P or R, so the biconditional is always true.

given.aim: Find the well-formed formula that is true under every interpretation.

terms.meaning: binary predicates over the domain
terms.connects: atoms inside the quantified formulas

terms.meaning: two forms of the same truth function
terms.example: both false only when P true and R false
terms.connects: the core of option C

terms.meaning: the shared quantifier prefix in C
terms.example: for each x there is a y ...
terms.connects: wraps both equivalent matrices identically

terms.meaning: a formula true in every model
terms.connects: the property we are testing each option for

given.plan: Test each option for validity; recognise that only C substitutes a logically equivalent matrix under an identical prefix, so only C is a tautology.

steps.title: Reject options that reorder quantifiers
steps.formula_raw: forall x exists y R(x,y) does NOT imply exists y forall x R(x,y)
steps.apply: Option A claims a biconditional between forall-exists and exists-forall, which fails (e.g. R = equality on a 2-element set). So A is not a tautology.
steps.note: exists-forall implies forall-exists, but not the reverse.

steps.title: Reject B and D
steps.apply: In B, the antecedent's R(x,y) and S(x,y) share a free y under forall x [exists y R -> S], but the consequent re-quantifies, so a model can make antecedent true and consequent false. In D, swapping P(x,y) to P(y,x) under forall x forall y is only valid because both range over all pairs; however D is not in biconditional form and the intended tautology is the airtight C.
steps.note: Only one option is the cleanest universally-true wff.

steps.title: Verify C with material implication
steps.apply: Inside the SAME prefix forall x exists y, the matrix P(x,y) -> R(x,y) equals ~P(x,y) or R(x,y) in every interpretation, so the two sides of the biconditional always agree.
steps.note: Substituting a logical equivalent under any prefix preserves equivalence.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Because both sides of C are forced to have the same truth value in every model, C is a tautology.
steps.note: C is the answer.

questions.formula_note: The identity P -> Q = ~P or Q, applied under one fixed quantifier prefix, makes both sides of option C always equal -- the definition of a tautology.

questions.question: Which one of the following well-formed formulae in predicate calculus is NOT valid ? A. (forall x p(x) -> forall x q(x)) -> (exists x ~p(x) or forall x q(x))  B. (exists x p(x) or exists x q(x)) -> exists x (p(x) or q(x))  C. exists x (p(x) and q(x)) -> (exists x p(x) and exists x q(x))  D. forall x (p(x) or q(x)) -> (forall x p(x) or forall x q(x))
questions.answer: D. forall x (p(x) or q(x)) -> (forall x p(x) or forall x q(x))
questions.to_find: Which predicate-calculus formula is NOT valid

understand.plain: A valid formula is true in every interpretation. The trap is option D: "every element is p or q" does NOT force "all are p OR all are q", because the choice of p vs q can differ from element to element. The other three are standard valid distribution/implication laws.

keywords.explain: A predicate-calculus formula is valid if it is true under every domain and interpretation.
keywords.example: forall x p(x) -> exists x p(x) is valid for non-empty domains.

keywords.term: forall does NOT distribute over or
keywords.explain: forall x (p or q) is weaker than (forall x p) or (forall x q).
keywords.example: Even numbers are even-or-odd, but not all even nor all odd.

keywords.explain: exists x (p or q) is equivalent to (exists x p) or (exists x q), so B is valid.
keywords.example: Some number is prime or composite splits cleanly.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='500' height='180' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='30' y='42'>Domain = {a, b}</text><circle cx='120' cy='100' r='30' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='108' y='106'>a</text><text class='sub mut' x='95' y='150'>p(a) true</text><circle cx='340' cy='100' r='30' class='hi'/><text class='ink lbl' x='328' y='106'>b</text><text class='sub mut' x='325' y='150'>q(b) true</text><text class='ink sub' x='30' y='178'>forall x (p or q) holds, yet neither forall p nor forall q holds</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: On a two-element domain where a satisfies p and b satisfies q, every element is p-or-q but not all are p and not all are q, refuting option D.

given.aim: Identify the predicate-calculus formula that fails to be valid (true in every interpretation).

terms.meaning: unary predicates over the domain
terms.connects: the atoms combined by and / or in each option

terms.meaning: every element satisfies p or q (choice may vary)
terms.example: each integer is even or odd
terms.connects: the weak antecedent in option D

terms.meaning: either ALL satisfy p, or ALL satisfy q
terms.example: all integers even, or all odd -- false
terms.connects: the strong consequent D wrongly claims

terms.meaning: a domain + interpretation making the formula false
terms.connects: demonstrates D is not valid

given.plan: Confirm A, B, C are valid distribution/implication laws, then build a counterexample model showing D fails.

steps.title: Check the valid distribution laws
steps.formula_raw: exists x (p or q) = (exists x p) or (exists x q)
steps.apply: Option B is exactly this equivalence (one direction), so B is valid. Option C is valid too: if some single x makes p(x) and q(x) true, that x witnesses both exists x p and exists x q.
steps.note: exists distributes over or; a common witness gives both existentials.

steps.title: Check option A
steps.apply: Rewrite forall x q(x) as Q and forall x p(x) as P. A says (P -> Q) -> (~forall x p(x) or Q) = (P->Q) -> (~P or Q). Since P->Q already equals ~P or Q, the consequent is implied; A is valid.
steps.note: exists x ~p(x) is ~forall x p(x) = ~P.

steps.title: Test option D with a model
steps.formula_raw: forall x (p(x) or q(x)) does NOT imply (forall x p(x)) or (forall x q(x))
steps.apply: Take domain {a, b} with p(a) true, q(a) false, p(b) false, q(b) true. Then forall x (p or q) is true, but forall x p is false (b fails) and forall x q is false (a fails), so the consequent is false. The implication is false here.
steps.note: The per-element choice of p or q breaks the distribution.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.formula_raw: a single falsifying model shows non-validity
steps.apply: Because D is false in at least one interpretation, D is NOT valid.
steps.note: D is the answer.

solution.result: D is the formula that is NOT valid.

questions.formula_note: The key fact is that forall does not distribute over or: forall x (p or q) is strictly weaker than (forall x p) or (forall x q), so option D fails.

questions.question: Consider the first-order logic sentence F : forall x (exists y R(x,y)). Assuming non-empty logical domains, which of the sentences below are implied by F? I. exists y (exists x R(x,y))  II. exists y (forall x R(x,y))  III. forall y (exists x R(x,y))  IV. ~exists x (forall y ~R(x,y))  A. IV only  B. I and IV only  C. II only  D. II and III only
questions.to_find: Which listed sentences are logically implied by F : forall x exists y R(x,y)

understand.plain: F says every x has some partner y with R(x,y). From this you can deduce that at least one related pair exists (sentence I) and you can re-derive F itself in negated form (sentence IV). But F does NOT give one universal y (II) nor cover every y as a target (III), so those are not implied.

keywords.explain: forall x exists y R(x,y) means each x has its own (possibly different) y.
From forall x exists y R, with a non-empty domain, pick any x to get exists x exists y R, i.e. exists y exists x R.
keywords.example: if everyone has a mother, then somebody-mother pair exists.

keywords.explain: ~exists x (forall y ~R(x,y)) equals forall x ~(forall y ~R(x,y)) = forall x exists y R(x,y) = F.
keywords.example: "no x is unrelated to every y" is just F restated.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class='bg' x='10' y='10' width='520' height='200' rx='10'/><text class='lbl ink' x='30' y='40'>F: each x picks some y</text><circle cx='110' cy='90' r='22' class='hi'/><text class='ink sub' x='102' y='95'>x1</text><circle cx='110' cy='160' r='22' class='hi'/><text class='ink sub' x='102' y='165'>x2</text><circle cx='400' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text class='ink sub' x='392' y='95'>y1</text><circle cx='400' cy='160' r='22' class='bg'/><text class='ink sub' x='392' y='165'>y2</text><line class='edge' x1='132' y1='90' x2='378' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='132' y1='160' x2='378' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text class='sub mut' x='30' y='200'>Different y per x: no single y works for all (II false); y could be unused (III false)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A bipartite picture: each x on the left has its own arrow to some y on the right, so a related pair exists (I) but no single y serves all x (II) and some y may be untargeted (III).

given.aim: Decide which of sentences I-IV are logically implied by F : forall x exists y R(x,y).

terms.term: F : forall x exists y R(x,y)
terms.meaning: every x has at least one related y
terms.example: every node has an outgoing edge
terms.connects: the premise from which we test implications

terms.term: I : exists y exists x R(x,y)
terms.meaning: some related pair (x,y) exists
terms.connects: follows from F on a non-empty domain

terms.term: II : exists y forall x R(x,y)
terms.meaning: one common y related to every x
terms.example: a universal sink targeted by all
terms.connects: stronger than F, not implied

terms.meaning: no x is unrelated to all y -- equals F
terms.connects: logically identical to F, so implied

given.plan: Show I and IV follow from F (existential weakening and double-negation rewriting), and give models where II and III fail.

steps.title: I is implied (existential weakening)
steps.formula_raw: forall x exists y R(x,y) -> exists x exists y R(x,y) (non-empty domain)
steps.apply: Pick any element a (domain non-empty); F gives exists y R(a,y), hence exists x exists y R(x,y) = exists y exists x R(x,y). So I holds.
steps.note: Reordering two existentials is always allowed.

steps.title: IV is implied (it equals F)
steps.formula_raw: ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x); ~forall y ~R = exists y R
steps.apply: ~exists x (forall y ~R(x,y)) = forall x ~(forall y ~R(x,y)) = forall x (exists y R(x,y)) = F. So IV is logically equivalent to F and is implied.
steps.note: Double negation: ~(forall y ~R) = exists y R.

steps.title: II is NOT implied
steps.formula_raw: forall x exists y R does NOT imply exists y forall x R
steps.apply: Model: domain {1,2}, R(1,1) and R(2,2) only. Each x has a partner (F true), but no single y is related to both 1 and 2, so exists y forall x R fails. II not implied.
steps.note: forall-exists is weaker than exists-forall.

steps.title: III is NOT implied
steps.formula_raw: F says nothing about every y being a target
steps.apply: Same model {1,2} with R(1,1),R(2,2): take y not equal to any second coordinate, or note R(_,1) holds (x=1) and R(_,2) holds (x=2) here so adjust -- use domain {1,2,3}, R(1,1),R(2,2),R(3,1); then y=3 has no x with R(x,3), so forall y exists x R fails. III not implied.
steps.note: Some y can be untargeted, breaking III.

solution.result: Only I and IV are implied by F -- option B.

questions.formula_note: IV is just F rewritten via De Morgan for quantifiers, and I is F weakened to an existential; II and III demand quantifier strength F does not provide, so the answer is I and IV only (B).

formulas.name: Negation of Quantifiers (De Morgan for Quantifiers)
formulas.latex:
eg orall x\,P(x)\equiv\exists x\,
eg P(x),\quad 
eg\exists x\,P(x)\equiv orall x\,
eg P(x)
formulas.plain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
formulas.whenToUse: When pushing a negation across a quantifier; converts "not all" into "some not" and "none" into "all not".

terms.means: universal quantifier: for every element x
existential quantifier: for at least one element x

formulas.trap: Each negation flips the quantifier; do not forget to also negate the inner predicate body.
formulas.reference: First-order logic: rules of inference and equivalences

formulas.name: Negation of an Implication
formulas.whenToUse: When negating a conditional, especially inside a negated universal to model "some A is not B".
formulas.trap: The negation is a conjunction (P and ~Q), not another implication; the antecedent stays positive.

formulas.name: Negated Conjunction as Implication
formulas.whenToUse: When a negated AND must be rewritten as an implication to compare with answer choices.
formulas.trap: This is De Morgan (~A or ~B) re-expressed as A -> ~B; the equivalent contrapositive is B -> ~A.

formulas.name: Material Implication
formulas.whenToUse: To rewrite any implication as a disjunction (or vice versa) when simplifying or comparing formulas.
formulas.trap: Only the antecedent is negated; P -> Q is NOT P or ~Q.

formulas.name: Contrapositive Equivalence
formulas.whenToUse: To recognise an equivalent restatement of a conditional and to distinguish it from the (non-equivalent) converse and inverse.
formulas.trap: The converse Q -> P and inverse ~P -> ~Q are NOT equivalent to P -> Q.

formulas.name: Categorical "All A are B"
formulas.plain: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
formulas.whenToUse: When translating an English universal categorical statement into first-order logic.

terms.means: the predicate class asserted of every A

formulas.trap: Use implication, not conjunction; forall x (A and B) wrongly claims everything is both A and B.
formulas.reference: First-order logic translation of categorical statements

formulas.name: Tautology / Valid wff
formulas.plain: A formula is a tautology (valid) iff it is true under every interpretation/model.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which formula is always true regardless of the domain or predicate meanings.
formulas.trap: One falsifying interpretation is enough to show a formula is not a tautology.

formulas.name: Quantifier Order (forall-exists vs exists-forall)
formulas.latex: \exists y\forall x\,R(x,y)\rightarrow\forall x\exists y\,R(x,y)\ \text{(one way only)}
formulas.plain: exists y forall x R implies forall x exists y R, but NOT conversely
formulas.whenToUse: When swapping the order of a universal and existential quantifier; only the exists-forall to forall-exists direction is valid.

terms.means: each x may have its own y (weaker)
one fixed y works for all x (stronger)

formulas.trap: Do not assume forall x exists y R(x,y) gives a single common y; that is the stronger exists-forall form.
formulas.reference: First-order logic: quantifier scope and order

formulas.name: Existential Distributes over Disjunction
formulas.plain: exists x (P or Q) = (exists x P) or (exists x Q)
formulas.whenToUse: When moving an existential quantifier across an OR (it distributes both ways).
formulas.trap: exists does NOT distribute over AND in general; only over OR.

formulas.name: Universal Does NOT Distribute over Disjunction
formulas.plain: forall x (P or Q) does NOT equal (forall x P) or (forall x Q)
formulas.whenToUse: To spot an invalid distribution of a universal quantifier over OR.

terms.means: each element satisfies P or Q (choice may vary)
all satisfy P, or all satisfy Q (stronger)

formulas.trap: The per-element choice can alternate, so the right side can be false while the left is true.

formulas.name: Existential Weakening from a Universal (non-empty domain)
formulas.plain: On a non-empty domain, forall x exists y R(x,y) implies exists x exists y R(x,y)
formulas.whenToUse: When deducing that at least one instance/pair exists from a universal statement over a non-empty domain.
formulas.trap: Requires the domain to be non-empty; over an empty domain a universal is vacuously true but no instance exists.
